message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T96 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Branch: implement/T96    Base commit: a146fde (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard a146fde` first; confirm TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant exists (internal/fec or internal/bind) + the T91/T93 per-peer fecSend/fecRecv split before starting.
headline: Re-run FEC prefix-stability and FEC suite after per-peer FEC split
description: Confirm the per-peer fecSend/fecRecv split preserves the Reed-Solomon invariants: run TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant and the FEC datapath suite, and add a per-peer FEC recovery assertion (one peer's parity never reconstructs into another peer's decoder). Fix any regression surfaced.
acceptance: `go test ./internal/... -run FEC` and TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant pass; a new assertion shows peer A's parity shards never feed peer B's decoder.

This is primarily a TEST/verification task. Locate TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant (grep) + the FEC datapath suite and confirm they pass post-split. Then ADD the per-peer FEC-isolation assertion: peer A's PARITY shards (encoded under A's psk) must NEVER reconstruct into peer B's decoder (build on the T91/T93 per-peer fecSend/fecRecv machinery + the T89/R93 FEC-arming seam). Mutation-verify it discriminates. If the split introduced a REAL FEC regression, commit the failing test as a repro and report it in blockedReason rather than masking it.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./internal/... -run FEC && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/fec + internal/bind (peer_fec_lifecycle_test.go for the FEC-enabled per-peer fixtures).
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t96-fix` off a146fde in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"bytes"
5		"io"
6		"net/netip"
7		"sync"
8		"testing"
9		"time"
10	
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
12		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
13		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/frame"
14		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
15		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
16		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
17	)
18	
19	// newProbingMultipathFEC is newProbingMultipath with a FEC config threaded through NewMultipath,
20	// so a concentrator peer bound lazily over its sockets re-instantiates its FULL heavy trio
21	// (resequencer + FEC DECODE and FEC ENCODE planes) on binding — the FEC-on lifecycle the T91
22	// FEC-off fixtures could not exercise (every prior fixture passed nil fecCfg, making the
23	// 'fecRecv nil / fecSend nil' assertions vacuous).
24	func newProbingMultipathFEC(t testing.TB, paths []config.Path, psk config.Key, fecCfg *fec.Config, clk telemetry.Clock) (*Multipath, []*telemetry.Prober) {
25		t.Helper()
26		lg, err := log.New("error", io.Discard)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build logger: %v", err)
29		}
30		cfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
31			LossWindow: 0,
32			Liveness:   telemetry.LivenessConfig{DownAfter: testProbeDownAfter, UpAfterSuccesses: testProbeUpSucc},
33		}
34		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
35			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, testProbeSessionID, psk, cfg, clk, lg)
36		}
37		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(paths))
38		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(paths))
39		for i := range paths {
40			probers[i] = newProber(paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
41			health[i] = probers[i]
42		}
43		scheduler, err := sched.NewActiveBackup(health, sched.Config{FailbackAfter: time.Hour}, clk, lg)
44		if err != nil {
45			t.Fatalf("build scheduler: %v", err)
46		}
47		m, err := NewMultipath(paths, psk, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecCfg, nil, config.Amnezia{})
48		if err != nil {
49			t.Fatalf("NewMultipath: %v", err)
50		}
51		return m, probers
52	}
53	
54	// lazyConcentratorFEC is lazyConcentrator with FEC configured: a one-shared-socket Multipath
55	// whose primary is keyed by pskA and whose SECOND peer (pskB) is bound LAZILY (no heavy state
56	// yet). Because fecCfg is non-nil, ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated builds the peer's FEC decode
57	// AND encode planes on binding (and teardownPeerLocked frees them), so the FEC-plane lifecycle
58	// asserted below is driven by PRODUCTION code, not the fixture.
59	func lazyConcentratorFEC(t *testing.T, pskA, pskB config.Key, fecCfg *fec.Config) (m *Multipath, primary, second *peerState, clk *fakeClock) {
60		t.Helper()
61		clk = newFakeClock()
62		m, _ = newProbingMultipathFEC(t, loopbackPaths(1), pskA, fecCfg, clk)
63		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
64			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
65		}
66		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
67		primary = m.peerState
68		second = bindLazyPeer(t, m, "peer-b", pskB, clk)
69		if peerPathByName(second, "a") == nil {
70			t.Fatalf("second peer has no view of shared path 'a': %v", pathNamesOfPeer(second))
71		}
72		return m, primary, second, clk
73	}
74	
75	// offerReconstructingParity delivers, through the demux, a GENUINE single-frame-group parity
76	// (DataCount=1) from a source ALREADY BOUND to peer p, so it reaches p's FEC decoder via
77	// dispatchInbound's PARITY case. Offered alone, that parity reconstructs its sole data shard,
78	// so the decoder's Recovered counter must advance by one — the observable that a re-instantiated
79	// FEC decode plane is not merely present but actually recovering. It returns the decoder's
80	// Recovered delta.
81	func offerReconstructingParity(t *testing.T, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, codec *frame.Codec, src netip.AddrPort, cfg fec.Config, seq uint64, inner string) uint64 {
82		t.Helper()
83		fr := view.peer.fecRecv.Load()
84		if fr == nil {
85			t.Fatal("offerReconstructingParity: peer has no FEC decoder armed")
86		}
87		before := fr.stats().Recovered
88		group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, cfg, seq, []byte(inner))
89		parity, err := codec.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: view.id, Payload: parityPayload})
90		if err != nil {
91			t.Fatalf("encode reconstructing parity: %v", err)
92		}
93		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, src)
94		return fr.stats().Recovered - before
95	}
96	
97	// TestConcentratorFECReceivePlaneLifecycle is the FEC-ENABLED T91 receive-plane acceptance
98	// (round-2 hardening): a configured concentrator peer's FEC DECODER is ABSENT before its first
99	// authenticated binding, INSTANTIATED on that binding and actually RECONSTRUCTING parity, FREED
100	// on teardown, and RE-INSTANTIATED as a FRESH decoder that reconstructs again after re-bind.
101	// Unlike the FEC-off fixtures, the 'fecRecv nil before / present after' assertions here are NOT
102	// vacuous: fecCfg is non-nil, so deleting ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated's fecRecv install leaves
103	// fecRecv nil after binding and this test red (mutation-verified).
104	func TestConcentratorFECReceivePlaneLifecycle(t *testing.T) {
105		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
106		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
107		fecCfg := &fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: testFECDeadline}
108		m, _, second, clk := lazyConcentratorFEC(t, pskA, pskB, fecCfg)
109		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
110		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
111		if err != nil {
112			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
113		}
114	
115		// (1) FEC decoder absent before the first authenticated binding.
116		if second.fecRecv.Load() != nil {
117			t.Fatal("a configured concentrator peer holds a FEC decoder before any binding (FEC receive not lazy)")
118		}
119	
120		// (2) Binding instantiates the FEC decoder, and it actually reconstructs a parity-only group.
121		src := synthSource(1)
122		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 1, clk), src)
123		fr := second.fecRecv.Load()
124		if fr == nil {
125			t.Fatal("peer B's FEC decoder was not instantiated on its first authenticated binding")
126		}
127		if got := offerReconstructingParity(t, m, secondView, codecB, src, *fecCfg, 1000, "recovered-1"); got != 1 {
128			t.Fatalf("freshly-instantiated FEC decoder recovered %d frames from a DataCount=1 parity, want 1", got)
129		}
130	
131		// (3) Teardown frees the FEC decoder.
132		if m.peerIsLiveLocked(second) {
133			t.Fatal("peer B is unexpectedly live before any echo was fed")
134		}
135		if !m.TearDownPeer("peer-b") {
136			t.Fatal("TearDownPeer refused to tear down a DEAD peer")
137		}
138		if second.fecRecv.Load() != nil {
139			t.Fatal("teardown did not free peer B's FEC decoder")
140		}
141	
142		// (4) Re-bind re-instantiates a FRESH decoder that reconstructs again.
143		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 2, clk), src)
144		fr2 := second.fecRecv.Load()
145		if fr2 == nil {
146			t.Fatal("peer B's FEC decoder was not re-instantiated on re-bind")
147		}
148		if fr2 == fr {
149			t.Fatal("re-bind reused the torn-down FEC decoder instance instead of a fresh one")
150		}
151		if got := offerReconstructingParity(t, m, secondView, codecB, src, *fecCfg, 2000, "recovered-2"); got != 1 {
152			t.Fatalf("re-instantiated FEC decoder recovered %d frames after re-bind, want 1", got)
153		}
154	}
155	
156	// TestConcentratorFECSendPlaneReinstantiatedOnRebind is the round-2 acceptance for the PRODUCTION
157	// DEFECT the panel found in T91's own 're-instantiate + pass traffic on re-bind' criterion: a
158	// torn-down concentrator peer's FEC SEND plane (fecSend) was freed on teardown but NEVER rebuilt
159	// on re-bind (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated was receive-only), so a rebound FEC-configured peer
160	// SENT WITHOUT PARITY until a full Close/Open. This asserts fecSend is absent → present on binding
161	// → freed on teardown → RE-INSTANTIATED (fresh) on re-bind, and that the rebound peer's Send
162	// actually EMITS PARITY again. Deleting the fecSend re-instantiation leaves this red.
163	func TestConcentratorFECSendPlaneReinstantiatedOnRebind(t *testing.T) {
164		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
165		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
166		// DataShards=4: one Send batch of 4 inner datagrams closes exactly one group, emitting parity.
167		fecCfg := &fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: testFECDeadline}
168		m, _, second, clk := lazyConcentratorFEC(t, pskA, pskB, fecCfg)
169		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
170	
171		// (1) FEC send plane absent before binding.
172		if second.fecSend.Load() != nil {
173			t.Fatal("a configured concentrator peer holds a FEC sender before any binding")
174		}
175	
176		// (2) Binding instantiates the FEC send plane.
177		src := synthSource(1)
178		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 1, clk), src)
179		fs := second.fecSend.Load()
180		if fs == nil {
181			t.Fatal("peer B's FEC sender was not instantiated on its first authenticated binding")
182		}
183	
184		// (3) Teardown frees the FEC send plane (dead peer: no echoes fed).
185		if !m.TearDownPeer("peer-b") {
186			t.Fatal("TearDownPeer refused to tear down a DEAD peer")
187		}
188		if second.fecSend.Load() != nil {
189			t.Fatal("teardown did not free peer B's FEC sender")
190		}
191	
192		// (4) Re-bind re-instantiates a FRESH FEC send plane — the fix for the production defect.
193		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 2, clk), src)
194		fs2 := second.fecSend.Load()
195		if fs2 == nil {
196			t.Fatal("re-bind did not re-instantiate peer B's FEC sender: a rebound FEC peer would send WITHOUT parity")
197		}
198		if fs2 == fs {
199			t.Fatal("re-bind reused the torn-down FEC sender instance instead of a fresh one")
200		}
201	
202		// (5) The rebound peer's Send actually EMITS PARITY again. Bring its path Up and give it a
203		// remote, then Send one full group's worth of inner datagrams; the encoder must close the
204		// group and egress parity, counted on fecSend.parityFrames.
205		raw, rawAP := rawPeer(t)
206		_ = raw
207		secondView.setRemote(rawAP)
208		driveConcentratorPathUp(t, secondView, pskB, clk)
209	
210		batch := [][]byte{[]byte("g0-0"), []byte("g0-1"), []byte("g0-2"), []byte("g0-3")}
211		if err := m.Send(batch, second.virt); err != nil {
212			t.Fatalf("Send through rebound peer B: %v", err)
213		}
214		if got := second.fecSend.Load().parityFrames.Load(); got == 0 {
215			t.Fatal("rebound FEC peer emitted NO parity frames on a full group: fecSend was not truly re-instantiated (production defect)")
216		}
217	}
218	
219	// TestDispatchInboundNilGuardsDropNotPanic is the deterministic white-box acceptance for the
220	// dispatchInbound nil-guards (multipath.go DATA case + PARITY case). It reproduces the exact
221	// TearDownPeer-vs-readLoop ordering the guards defend: bindSourceToPeer has already resolved a
222	// still-bound source to peer B (so demuxInbound routes straight to dispatchInbound WITHOUT
223	// re-checking the ring), and TearDownPeer Store(nil)s peer B's resequencer out from under the
224	// in-flight dispatch. The guards must DROP the frame rather than dereference the nil ring. Each
225	// subtest FAILS (panics on a nil-pointer dereference) when its guard is removed — the prior round's
226	// guard tests were vacuous because their post-teardown source was already UNBOUND, so the frame
227	// never reached dispatchInbound. FEC is ON so the PARITY guard (which lives inside the fecRecv!=nil
228	// branch) is actually exercised.
229	func TestDispatchInboundNilGuardsDropNotPanic(t *testing.T) {
230		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
231		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
232		fecCfg := &fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: testFECDeadline}
233	
234		t.Run("DATA to a bound peer whose resequencer was niled mid-flight is dropped without panic", func(t *testing.T) {
235			m, primary, second, clk := lazyConcentratorFEC(t, pskA, pskB, fecCfg)
236			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
237			dataCodecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
238			if err != nil {
239				t.Fatalf("build peer B data codec: %v", err)
240			}
241			src := synthSource(1)
242			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 1, clk), src)
243			if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(src.Addr()); !ok || bound != second {
244				t.Fatalf("probe did not bind source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
245			}
246			// The source stays BOUND; only the ring is torn out — the ordering the DATA guard defends.
247			second.resequencer.Store(nil)
248			// Must not panic: with FEC armed, removing `rq == nil { return }` reaches rq.Observe on the
249			// nil ring inside the fecRecv branch and panics.
250			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], mustEncodeData(t, dataCodecB, 100, secondView.id, "raced-data"), src)
251			// The frame was dropped, never misrouted into the primary.
252			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
253				t.Fatalf("raced DATA leaked into the primary ring (%q)", it.Payload)
254			}
255		})
256	
257		t.Run("PARITY to a bound peer with FEC armed but resequencer niled mid-flight is dropped without panic", func(t *testing.T) {
258			m, _, second, clk := lazyConcentratorFEC(t, pskA, pskB, fecCfg)
259			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
260			codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
261			if err != nil {
262				t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
263			}
264			src := synthSource(1)
265			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 1, clk), src)
266			if second.fecRecv.Load() == nil {
267				t.Fatal("peer B's FEC decoder was not armed after binding")
268			}
269			// Nil the ring but LEAVE fecRecv armed: the PARITY guard lives inside the fecRecv!=nil
270			// branch. A DataCount=1 parity reconstructs its sole shard, so removing `rq == nil { return }`
271			// drives observeRecovered -> rq.ObserveRecovered on the nil ring and panics.
272			second.resequencer.Store(nil)
273			group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, *fecCfg, 55, []byte("raced-parity"))
274			parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: parityPayload})
275			if err != nil {
276				t.Fatalf("encode parity: %v", err)
277			}
278			// Must not panic: the guard drops the reconstruction rather than resequencing on a nil ring.
279			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, src)
280		})
281	}
282	
283	// TestConcentratorTeardownRebindDemuxRace drives the CONCURRENT ordering the dispatchInbound
284	// nil-guards and the per-peer lifecycleMu exist for: the peer's SINGLE per-path readLoop resolves
285	// a still-bound source and dispatches DATA/PARITY (and re-binds, re-instantiating the planes) WHILE
286	// TearDownPeer — driven from the device's session-event goroutine — Store(nil)s those planes. It is
287	// exactly one demux driver against one teardown driver, matching production's one-readLoop-per-path
288	// discipline (a frame.Codec is single-goroutine by design; two concurrent demuxes on one path would
289	// be a test artifact, not a real race). Under -race it proves (a) no data race across the atomic
290	// trio nor the lifecycleMu-ordered (re)instantiation-vs-teardown, and (b) no nil-dereference panic —
291	// the DATA case's `rq == nil` guard and the PARITY case's guard drop a frame whose ring was niled
292	// mid-flight rather than dereferencing it. FEC is on so BOTH dispatch guards are live.
293	func TestConcentratorTeardownRebindDemuxRace(t *testing.T) {
294		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11)
295		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22)
296		fecCfg := &fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: testFECDeadline}
297		m, _, second, clk := lazyConcentratorFEC(t, pskA, pskB, fecCfg)
298		secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
299		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
300		if err != nil {
301			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
302		}
303		dataCodecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
304		if err != nil {
305			t.Fatalf("build peer B data codec: %v", err)
306		}
307		src := synthSource(1)
308	
309		const rounds = 400
310		for i := 0; i < rounds; i++ {
311			// Precompute every wire serially in the test goroutine (the fake clock and the stateful
312			// codecs are NOT concurrency-safe), so the demux goroutine below only touches the
313			// concurrent targets under test: m.demuxInbound (a single per-path reader) and, on the
314			// other goroutine, m.TearDownPeer.
315			probeWire := authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, uint64(2*i+2), clk)
316			dataWire := mustEncodeData(t, dataCodecB, uint64(1000+i), secondView.id, "d")
317			group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, *fecCfg, uint64(5000+i), []byte("p"))
318			parityWire, perr := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: parityPayload})
319			if perr != nil {
320				t.Fatalf("encode parity: %v", perr)
321			}
322	
323			var wg sync.WaitGroup
324			wg.Add(2)
325			// The one per-path reader: re-bind (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated), then dispatch DATA and
326			// PARITY through the two nil-guards, all on ONE goroutine as a real readLoop would.
327			go func() {
328				defer wg.Done()
329				m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeWire, src)
330				m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], dataWire, src)
331				m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parityWire, src)
332			}()
333			// The session-event driver: teardown — Store(nil)s the heavy trio under m.mu+lifecycleMu,
334			// racing the reader's dispatch and re-instantiation.
335			go func() {
336				defer wg.Done()
337				m.TearDownPeer("peer-b")
338			}()
339			wg.Wait()
340		}
341	
342		// Positive postcondition: after all the churn the peer still re-binds cleanly and carries
343		// DATA — the guards drop raced frames, they do not wedge the datapath.
344		m.TearDownPeer("peer-b") // ensure a known torn-down starting point (no-op if already down)
345		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], authProbe(t, pskB, secondView.id, 999999, clk), src)
346		if second.resequencer.Load() == nil {
347			t.Fatal("peer B did not re-instantiate its ring after the concurrent churn")
348		}
349		m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], mustEncodeData(t, dataCodecB, 888888, secondView.id, "final"), src)
350		if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("final")) {
351			t.Fatalf("peer B did not carry DATA after the concurrent churn: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
352		}
353	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"encoding/binary"
5		"fmt"

10		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"

15	// FEC datapath integration (T24).
16	//
17	// The fixed-ratio Reed-Solomon engine (internal/fec) is wired into the multipath
18	// datapath as a SEND-side encoder and a RECEIVE-side decoder that composes with the
19	// T18 resequencer, T21 weighted scheduling, and T30 dynamic paths without regressing
20	// any of them. FEC is OFF unless a config carries an [fec] block (NewMultipath's
21	// fecCfg is nil otherwise), in which case the datapath is byte-for-byte the pre-T24
22	// behaviour.
23	//
24	// Wire mapping. The FEC data-shard bytes are OuterSeq || inner: the encoder codes
25	// parity over that, but the 8-byte outer-seq prefix is NOT transmitted — the DATA
26	// frame carries the outer-seq in its header and the inner datagram in its payload,
27	// and both sender and receiver reconstitute the shard bytes as
28	// header-seq || frame-payload. This keeps a recovered shard SELF-DESCRIBING (its
29	// reconstructed bytes carry its own outer-seq, so a frame reconstructed from parity
30	// resequences at its ORIGINAL outer-seq even when EVERY data frame of the group was
31	// lost) while adding only one wire byte per DATA frame (the shard index in the DATA
32	// header) and the group cardinality M per PARITY frame. See internal/frame.
33	//
34	// Concurrency. The encoder rides the Send path, already serialized under m.mu, plus
35	// a single deadline-tick goroutine that TryLocks m.mu (never blocking, so it can
36	// never deadlock Close's readersWG.Wait held under m.mu — the tickLivenessFromReceive
37	// discipline). The decoder is fed from the per-path readLoop goroutines (many
38	// concurrent), so it is guarded by its own mutex, disjoint from m.mu and from the
39	// resequencer's — matching the resequencer's lock discipline (never held across a
40	// syscall). Recovered frames are handed to the resequencer exactly like a
41	// natively-received frame, so FEC recovery slots in strictly BEFORE resequencing.
42	
43	// fecSeqPrefixLen is the width of the big-endian outer-seq prefix that heads each
44	// FEC data-shard's coded bytes. It is reconstructed from the DATA frame header on
45	// both ends and never travels the wire (see the wire mapping above).
46	const fecSeqPrefixLen = 8
47	
48	// maxFECDeadline is the authoritative upper bound on the FEC group-close deadline,
49	// coupling it to the receive resequencer's per-gap timeout (T24, defect #4). A group
50	// flushed by the deadline emits its parity `deadline` after the group opened; the
51	// reconstructed frames must reach the resequencer BEFORE it skips the gap
52	// (resequencerTimeout after the gap forms), or recovery is structurally too late — the
53	// gap is skipped first and the recovered frame dropped as past the release point. Held
54	// at half the resequencer timeout, the deadline leaves the other half for propagation
55	// and reconstruction. config validation enforces a matching load-time bound; this is
56	// the authoritative guard for any fec.Config built directly.
57	const maxFECDeadline = resequencerTimeout / 2
58	
59	// fecRetainGroups bounds the decoder's retained-group window (SetRetainWindow): a
60	// group more than this many group-ids behind the newest offered group is evicted,
61	// which is also when an incomplete group is finally accounted unrecoverable. It is
62	// sized well above the reorder/loss span a single group can straddle so a group is
63	// never evicted while its own shards are still legitimately in flight, yet small
64	// enough that an unrecoverable group is accounted promptly rather than after a
65	// 4096-group default. A group spans a handful of outer-seqs, so this comfortably
66	// exceeds the resequencerWindow's reordering budget in group units.
67	const fecRetainGroups = 512
68	
69	// fecResidualLossWindow is the trailing outer-seq width of the receive-side residual-
70	// loss estimator (T29): the post-FEC-recovery loss the P4 acceptance bounds. It is
71	// deliberately WIDER than the telemetry default (512) for two reasons: (1) resolution —
72	// the P4 bound is 0.5%, so the window must resolve finer than 1/512≈0.2%; 8192 gives
73	// ~0.012% granularity, well under the bound; (2) stability — averaging the residual over
74	// ~thousands of groups smooths the per-group binomial variance into a steady-state figure.
75	// It stays far below the resequencer window's reordering budget, and is wide enough that a
76	// recovered frame arriving a group or two after its gap still retroactively fills it, so
77	// genuinely-unrecovered seqs (whole-group and beyond-budget losses) are the only residual.
78	const fecResidualLossWindow = 8192
79	
80	// adaptiveControlInterval throttles how often the FEC tick loop folds a fresh loss
81	// sample into the adaptive controller (T29). It matches the probe cadence — the rate at
82	// which per-path loss estimates actually refresh — so the controller's EWMA sees ~one
83	// sample per probe interval as its tuning (internal/adaptivefec) assumes, regardless of
84	// the (possibly much smaller) FEC group-close deadline the tick loop runs at.
85	const adaptiveControlInterval = telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval
86	
87	// fecSender is the send-side FEC state: the group-forming encoder (accessed only
88	// under the Bind's m.mu, on the Send path and the deadline-tick goroutine) plus the
89	// parity-overhead counters the /metrics exposition reports. The counters are
90	// atomics read lock-free at snapshot time, mirroring the T23 per-path byte counters.
91	type fecSender struct {
92		enc *fec.Encoder
93	
94		// ctrl is the adaptive-FEC controller (T29), nil in fixed-ratio mode. It is driven
95		// (Observe) and read (Parity) ONLY from under the Bind's m.mu — the FEC tick loop's
96		// throttled drive and, transitively, the Send path — so it needs no lock of its own,
97		// matching the encoder's single-writer discipline. lastControlTick/haveControlTick
98		// throttle the drive to adaptiveControlInterval and are likewise m.mu-guarded.
99		ctrl            *adaptivefec.Controller
100		lastControlTick time.Time
101		haveControlTick bool
102	
103		dataFrames   atomic.Uint64
104		dataBytes    atomic.Uint64
105		parityFrames atomic.Uint64
106		parityBytes  atomic.Uint64
107	}
108	
109	// fecReceiver is the receive-side FEC state: the recovery decoder guarded by its own
110	// mutex (the per-path readLoop goroutines feed it concurrently). deliveredRecovered is
111	// the HONEST recovery count for /metrics — data frames a reconstruction actually
112	// delivered to the resequencer AHEAD of its release point (not merely reconstructed:
113	// a frame rebuilt after the resequencer already skipped its gap is dropped as late and
114	// must not be counted, else /metrics overstates recovery). It is an atomic so the
115	// snapshot reads it lock-free.
116	type fecReceiver struct {
117		mu  sync.Mutex
118		dec *fec.Decoder
119	
120		deliveredRecovered atomic.Uint64
121	
122		// connLoss is the POST-FEC-RECOVERY residual-loss estimator (T29, the P4 signal):
123		// every natively-received DATA outer-seq AND every FEC-reconstructed outer-seq is
124		// Observed into it, so its Loss() fraction is exactly the connection-scoped loss that
125		// survived FEC — outer-seqs neither received nor recovered. It is internally
126		// synchronized (its own mutex), fed concurrently by the per-path readLoop goroutines
127		// exactly like the resequencer, and read lock-free-enough at scrape time.
128		connLoss *telemetry.ConnLoss
129	}
130	
131	// offer feeds one surviving shard into the decoder and returns any data payloads it
132	// reconstructed (never-received frames). It holds the decoder mutex only for the
133	// in-memory reconstruct — never across a syscall — so it never blocks the send path.
134	func (r *fecReceiver) offer(s fec.Shard) ([]fec.Recovered, error) {
135		r.mu.Lock()
136		defer r.mu.Unlock()
137		return r.dec.Offer(s)
138	}
139	
140	// stats snapshots the decoder's cumulative recovery counters under its mutex.
141	func (r *fecReceiver) stats() fec.DecoderStats {
142		r.mu.Lock()
143		defer r.mu.Unlock()
144		return r.dec.Stats()
145	}
146	
147	// FECStats is a consistent snapshot of the Bind's FEC counters (T24), the shape the
148	// metrics.Source adapter maps onto the connection-scoped /metrics FEC series.
149	// DataFrames is the send-side DATA-frame count (the fixed-ratio overhead's
150	// denominator); ParityFrames/ParityBytes are the send-side overhead (parity emitted);
151	// Recovered and Unrecoverable are the receive-side outcome (data shards reconstructed
152	// from parity, and data shards lost beyond repair capacity). FEC being
153	// connection-scoped, these carry no path label. The frame overhead the operator cares
154	// about is ParityFrames/DataFrames, which tends to ParityShards/DataShards (M/K) once
155	// groups fill; both are counted only for frames that actually reached the socket, so
156	// the ratio reflects wire cost actually spent.
157	type FECStats struct {
158		DataFrames    uint64
159		DataBytes     uint64
160		ParityFrames  uint64
161		ParityBytes   uint64
162		Recovered     uint64
163		Unrecoverable uint64
164		// ResidualLoss is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
165		// (T29): the share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from
166		// parity. It is the P4 acceptance signal — the loss FEC did not mask. Zero when FEC is
167		// disabled or no data has flowed.
168		ResidualLoss float64
169	}
170	
171	// fecShardPayload builds the FEC data-shard coded bytes for outer-seq seq over the
172	// opaque inner datagram: seq (8 BE) || inner. The sender codes parity over this; the
173	// receiver reconstitutes the identical bytes from the DATA frame header + payload so
174	// the decoder's shards match what the encoder coded.
175	func fecShardPayload(seq uint64, inner []byte) []byte {
176		b := make([]byte, fecSeqPrefixLen+len(inner))
177		binary.BigEndian.PutUint64(b, seq)
178		copy(b[fecSeqPrefixLen:], inner)
179		return b
180	}
181	
182	// splitFECShardPayload inverts fecShardPayload, returning the outer-seq and a fresh
183	// copy of the inner datagram. It fails fast on a shard too short to hold the prefix,
184	// which would indicate a decoder/encoder mismatch rather than a recoverable
185	// condition. The inner copy lets the resequencer take ownership without retaining the
186	// larger coded-shard backing array.
187	func splitFECShardPayload(b []byte) (uint64, []byte, error) {
188		if len(b) < fecSeqPrefixLen {
189			return 0, nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: recovered FEC shard %d bytes, shorter than the %d-byte outer-seq prefix", len(b), fecSeqPrefixLen)
190		}
191		seq := binary.BigEndian.Uint64(b[:fecSeqPrefixLen])
192		inner := make([]byte, len(b)-fecSeqPrefixLen)
193		copy(inner, b[fecSeqPrefixLen:])
194		return seq, inner, nil
195	}

1330	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=false: an authenticated peer probe. Its source is learned as
1331	//     the path's remote (D11: a probe-only backup path gets a usable return remote
1332	//     before any DATA flows) and it is reflected straight back to that source via
1333	//     this path's socket (T13 Reflector). Reflection writes independently of the
1334	//     scheduler/getRemote so an echo returns even on a not-yet-active path.
1335	//   - PROBE, IsEcho=true: an authenticated echo of one of our own probes. Its
1336	//     source is learned as the remote too, and the raw echo is fed into this
1337	//     path's Prober (HandleEcho) to update RTT/loss and drive liveness.
1338	//   - anything else (PARITY/CONTROL/malformed): dropped.
1339	//
1340	// Remote-learning and reflection touch only authenticated (MAC-verified) PROBE
1341	// frames — Decode has already verified the tag for the PROBE kind — which is what
1342	// resolves D9: an attacker who forges an unauthenticated DATA frame can no longer
1343	// repoint a path's return endpoint.
1344	//
1345	// FEC seam (T24): DATA ingestion via resequencer.Observe is keyed purely on
1346	// outer-seq, so an FEC decoder can slot in BEFORE the resequencer with no change
1347	// here — PARITY (dropped today) will feed the decoder, which reconstructs missing
1348	// DATA frames and calls Observe with their ORIGINAL outer-seq, identical to a
1349	// natively-received frame.
1350	func (m *Multipath) handleInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1351		fr, err := ps.codec.Decode(raw)
1352		if err != nil {
1353			return // drop malformed / PSK-mismatched outer frames
1354		}
1355		m.dispatchInbound(ps, fr, raw, srcAP)
1356	}
1357	
1358	// demuxInbound is the readLoop's per-frame entry on a (possibly shared) socket: it resolves
1359	// which bound peer's view owns the datagram, then hands the frame to handleInbound on THAT
1360	// view (T88). Because the readLoop holds only the PRIMARY's view of a shared socket (one
1361	// reader per socket), the routing that a concentrator needs — a datagram from peer B decoded
1362	// and resequenced under peer B's plane — happens HERE, not by trusting the reader's own view.
1363	//
1364	// Fast path: a socket with a single peer view (the edge/hub, or any socket not shared by a
1365	// second peer) needs no demux — dispatch on the reader's own view, byte-identical to the
1366	// pre-concentrator behaviour. The per-socket view snapshot is read lock-free; a nil/one-entry
1367	// snapshot means "not a shared concentrator socket".
1368	//
1369	// Shared socket (>1 view):
1370	//   - A source already bound by a prior authenticated PROBE routes straight to that peer's
1371	//     view of THIS socket (one lock-free map Load). A frame that does not verify under that
1372	//     peer's psk — a spoofed source it cannot forge the MAC for — is dropped by handleInbound.
1373	//   - An unbound source is trial-decoded against each peer's psk-derived codec (O(peers),
1374	//     bounded by the static peer count). Only an authenticated PROBE establishes a binding
1375	//     (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned only from authenticated PROBEs), and a
1376	//     PROBE's MAC verifies under EXACTLY ONE psk — so the FIRST psk whose codec yields a PROBE
1377	//     identifies the peer, binds the source, dispatches, and the loop STOPS there. A non-PROBE
1378	//     decode does NOT stop the trial: DATA/PARITY carry no MAC and are forgeable by design, so
1379	//     a genuine PROBE from a later peer can cross-psk-garble into a DATA/PARITY kind under an
1380	//     earlier peer's codec (~0.4%); the loop must therefore `continue` past a non-PROBE decode
1381	//     to give every remaining psk its chance to authenticate a PROBE. A non-PROBE decode
1382	//     carries no binding authority and is dropped either way — a genuine DATA/PARITY from a
1383	//     not-yet-bound source never dispatches or binds until that peer's PROBE binds it, so
1384	//     continuing past it changes nothing for genuine frames. A forged/garbage frame verifies
1385	//     as a PROBE under NO psk, binds nothing, and is dropped cheaply.
1386	func (m *Multipath) demuxInbound(ps *peerPathState, raw []byte, srcAP netip.AddrPort) {
1387		views := ps.views.Load()
1388		if views == nil || len(*views) <= 1 {
1389			m.handleInbound(ps, raw, srcAP)
1390			return
1391		}
1392		if bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(srcAP.Addr()); ok {
1393			for _, v := range *views {
1394				if v.peer == bound {
1395					m.handleInbound(v, raw, srcAP)
1396					return
1397				}
1398			}
1399			return // the bound peer holds no view of this socket (removed): drop
1400		}
1401		for _, v := range *views {
1402			fr, err := v.codec.Decode(raw)
1403			if err != nil {
1404				continue // not this peer's psk — try the next
1405			}
1406			if _, isProbe := fr.(frame.Probe); !isProbe {
1407				// Decoded under this psk but not a PROBE (DATA/PARITY carry no MAC, so
1408				// this may be a genuine PROBE from a later peer that cross-garbled into an
1409				// unauthenticated kind here). No binding authority: try the remaining psks
1410				// rather than aborting — a genuine unbound DATA/PARITY still never binds.
1411				continue
1412			}
1413			if !m.bindSourceToPeer(srcAP.Addr(), v.peer) {
1414				// Provisional demux state is at its cap (bootstrap flood): drop this new source's
1415				// PROBE rather than grow the map or evict a live binding (Q26/Q27). WG retransmits
1416				// re-drive the bootstrap once a slot frees.
1417				return
1418			}
1419			// The binding just resolved this peer: lazily materialise its heavy receive datapath
1420			// (resequencer ring + FEC decoder buffers) BEFORE dispatch, so a configured peer that
1421			// had never been reached — and a peer whose state was torn down on session loss — pays
1422			// that memory only from its first authenticated binding onward (Q26).
1423			m.ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(v.peer)
1424			m.dispatchInbound(v, fr, raw, srcAP) // already decoded: dispatch without re-decoding
1425			return
1426		}
1427		// No peer's psk verified: a forged/garbage frame. No binding, drop.
1428	}

1430	// lookupPeerBySource resolves a learned source address to the peer bound to it, or nil when
1431	// the source is not yet bound. It reads the copy-on-write binding map with a single lock-free
1432	// Load, so the concentrator receive hot path never takes m.mu (see peerBySource).
1433	func (m *Multipath) lookupPeerBySource(addr netip.Addr) (*peerState, bool) {
1434		mp := m.peerBySource.Load()
1435		if mp == nil {
1436			return nil, false
1437		}
1438		p, ok := (*mp)[addr]
1439		return p, ok
1440	}

1444	// on m.mu (Close waits on the readers WHILE holding m.mu), so the binding — written from a

1446	// no-op, and a lost CAS (a concurrent bind on another socket) simply retries. Copy-on-write

1452	// maxDemuxSources cap and addr is a NEW source: drop-on-exhaustion (Q26/Q27). A cap-drop NEVER
1453	// evicts an existing binding, so a live peer is never disturbed by a bootstrap flood; only a

1456	// source does not grow the map and is therefore never blocked by the cap.

13	// fecReceiveConfig is the fixed-ratio group the strengthened PARITY subtest arms both
14	// peers' receivers with. K=1 parity is all that is needed: a DataCount=1 group (one data
15	// shard) reconstructs that sole data frame from the single parity shard ALONE, so a PARITY
16	// that leaks past the demux gate into dispatchInbound recovers a data frame straight into
17	// the dispatching peer's resequencer — which is what makes the leak observable.
18	var fecReceiveConfig = fec.Config{DataShards: 4, ParityShards: 1, Deadline: time.Millisecond}
19	
20	// enableFECReceive arms a peer's receive-side FEC through the SAME seam Open uses
21	// (openPeerDatapathLocked, multipath.go:851) to Store a fecReceiver. Without it the Parity
22	// case of dispatchInbound is a no-op (fecRecv nil), so a leaked PARITY would touch no
23	// resequencer and the drop property this subtest names would be unobservable.
24	func enableFECReceive(t testing.TB, p *peerState, cfg fec.Config) {
25		t.Helper()
26		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(cfg)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build FEC decoder: %v", err)
29		}
30		p.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
31	}
32	
33	// singleFrameParity builds a GENUINE parity shard for a one-data-frame group whose sole
34	// data shard's coded bytes are seq||inner (fecShardPayload) — the exact shape the datapath
35	// codes parity over. Offered to a decoder built at the same cfg, this parity ALONE
36	// reconstructs that data frame, so a PARITY carrying it would deliver seq||inner up a
37	// resequencer at outer-seq `seq`. It returns the wire fields for a frame.Parity.
38	func singleFrameParity(t testing.TB, cfg fec.Config, seq uint64, inner []byte) (group uint32, index uint16, dataCount uint8, payload []byte) {
39		t.Helper()
40		enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(cfg, fec.SystemClock{})
41		if err != nil {
42			t.Fatalf("build FEC encoder: %v", err)
43		}
44		if _, _, aerr := enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, inner)); aerr != nil {
45			t.Fatalf("admit single data shard: %v", aerr)
46		}
47		parity, err := enc.Flush() // close the 1-data group, coding K parity over its sole shard
48		if err != nil {
49			t.Fatalf("flush single-frame group: %v", err)
50		}
51		if len(parity) != cfg.ParityShards {
52			t.Fatalf("single-frame group emitted %d parity, want %d", len(parity), cfg.ParityShards)
53		}
54		pshard := parity[0]
55		if pshard.DataCount != 1 {
56			t.Fatalf("single-frame parity DataCount = %d, want 1", pshard.DataCount)
57		}
58		return uint32(pshard.Group), uint16(pshard.Index), uint8(pshard.DataCount), pshard.Payload
59	}

61	// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
62	// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
63	// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
64	// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
65	// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
66	// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
67	// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
68	// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
69	//
70	// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
71	// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
72	// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
73	func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
74		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
75		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
76	
77		// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
78		// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
79		// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
80		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
81		if err != nil {
82			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
83		}
84	
85		t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
86			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
87			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
88			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
89	
90			data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
91			if err != nil {
92				t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
93			}
94			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
95	
96			// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
97			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
98				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
99			}
100			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
101				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
102			}
103			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
104				t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
105			}
106		})
107	
108		t.Run("PARITY that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
109			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
110			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
111			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
112	
113			// Arm BOTH peers' receive-side FEC exactly as Open does (multipath.go:851). This is
114			// what makes the drop OBSERVABLE: with FEC receive off (the prior fixture), a PARITY
115			// leaked past the gate falls into dispatchInbound's fecRecv-nil no-op and touches no
116			// resequencer, so the subtest could not distinguish a gate that drops from one that
117			// dispatches. With FEC receive on, the DataCount=1 parity below reconstructs its sole
118			// data frame straight into the DISPATCHING peer's resequencer, so a leak is caught by
119			// both the decoder-recovery and the resequencer assertions (mutation-verified).
120			enableFECReceive(t, primary, fecReceiveConfig)
121			enableFECReceive(t, second, fecReceiveConfig)
122	
123			// A GENUINE single-frame-group parity: its one data shard's coded bytes are
124			// leakSeq||leakInner, so if this PARITY were dispatched into either peer's decoder it
125			// alone would recover that data frame and resequence leakInner at outer-seq leakSeq.
126			const leakSeq = 42
127			leakInner := []byte("parity-leak-recovers-this")
128			group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, fecReceiveConfig, leakSeq, leakInner)
129	
130			parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: parityPayload})
131			if err != nil {
132				t.Fatalf("encode PARITY under psk B: %v", err)
133			}
134			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, peerAP)
135	
136			// The gate dropped the unbound PARITY BEFORE dispatch: it reached NEITHER peer's FEC
137			// decoder (no reconstruction) NOR any resequencer. A dispatch-without-bind leak would
138			// trip whichever peer the leaked frame was dispatched to, so both are asserted.
139			for _, pc := range []struct {
140				who string
141				p   *peerState
142			}{{"peer B", second}, {"the PRIMARY", primary}} {
143				fr := pc.p.fecRecv.Load()
144				if got := fr.stats().Recovered; got != 0 {
145					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY reached %s's FEC decoder and reconstructed %d frame(s); a valid decode carries no binding authority and must not be dispatched", pc.who, got)
146				}
147				if got := fr.deliveredRecovered.Load(); got != 0 {
148					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY delivered %d reconstructed frame(s) up %s's resequencer", got, pc.who)
149				}
150				if it, ok := pc.p.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
151					t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in %s's resequencer (%q)", pc.who, it.Payload)
152				}
153			}
154			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
155				t.Fatal("an unbound PARITY frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
156			}
157		})
158	
159		t.Run("after an authenticated PROBE binds the source to B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only", func(t *testing.T) {
160			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
161			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
162			peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
163	
164			// Pre-binding DATA under B's psk from this source is dropped (the gate above).
165			early, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 4, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("early")})
166			if err != nil {
167				t.Fatalf("encode pre-bind DATA: %v", err)
168			}
169			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], early, peerAP)
170			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
171				t.Fatal("pre-bind DATA established a binding")
172			}
173	
174			// An authenticated PROBE under peer B's psk binds the source to peer B.
175			const seq = 7
176			ts := newFakeClock().Now().UnixNano()
177			probeRaw, err := frame.Encode(pskB, frame.Probe{PathID: secondView.id, ProbeSeq: seq, TimestampNanos: ts, IsEcho: false})
178			if err != nil {
179				t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk B: %v", err)
180			}
181			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeRaw, peerAP)
182	
183			bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr())
184			if !ok || bound != second {
185				t.Fatalf("PROBE did not bind the source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
186			}
187			// Drain peer B's reflected echo so a later timeout-read in the suite is unambiguous;
188			// its content is asserted by the T88 acceptance, so we only consume it here.
189			echoCodec, _ := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
190			_ = readProbe(t, peer, echoCodec)
191	
192			// Subsequent DATA from the now-bound source lands in peer B's resequencer, and ONLY B's.
193			late, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 9, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("late")})
194			if err != nil {
195				t.Fatalf("encode post-bind DATA: %v", err)
196			}
197			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], late, peerAP)
198			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("late")) {
199				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA did not reach peer B's resequencer: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
200			}
201			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
202				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA for peer B leaked into the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
203			}
204			// The pre-bind "early" frame was never buffered anywhere: peer B's resequencer held
205			// only "late" (already popped) and the primary's held nothing.
206			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
207				t.Fatalf("peer B's resequencer held a second frame (%q); the pre-bind DATA was not dropped", it.Payload)
208			}
209		})
210	}

937	// (derived from THIS peer's psk), its receive resequencer, and — when FEC is configured —
938	// its FEC send/receive planes. Each is fresh per Open so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect)
939	// re-pins the peer's release point and re-anchors its FEC grouping to this bring-up rather
940	// than a stale prior span. It writes ONLY the given peer's fields (from the peer's own psk
941	// and the bind-wide fecCfg/adaptiveCfg), so one peer's (re)creation never touches another
942	// peer's resequencer or FEC group state — the per-peer lifecycle boundary that keeps a
943	// reconnect on one peer from disturbing another's. Caller holds m.mu; on error the caller
944	// unwinds the whole Open via closeSocketsLocked (which clears every peer's per-Open state),
945	// so a partial build here is cleaned up.
946	func (m *Multipath) openPeerDatapathLocked(ps *peerState) error {
947		sendCodec, err := ps.newCodec()
948		if err != nil {
949			return err
950		}
951	
952		// A fresh resequencer per Open: its release point re-pins to the first frame THIS peer
953		// receives after this bring-up, so a Close→Open cycle (or reconnect) never wedges on a
954		// stale high-water outer-seq. Published atomically so the peer's per-path readLoop
955		// goroutines read it WITHOUT m.mu.
956		ps.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
957	
958		// Fresh FEC send/receive state per Open, when FEC is enabled (T24). The encoder
959		// group state and the decoder's per-group buffers re-pin with the sockets, so a
960		// Close→Open cycle never reconstructs against a stale group. Both are torn down (per
961		// peer) in closeSocketsLocked. A build error here is a programmer error (the ratio was
962		// validated in NewMultipath), so it fails the Open.
963		if m.fecCfg != nil {
964			fs, err := m.newFECSender()
965			if err != nil {
966				return err
967			}
968			ps.fecSend.Store(fs)
969			fr, err := m.newFECReceiver()
970			if err != nil {
971				return err
972			}
973			ps.fecRecv.Store(fr)
974		}
975		ps.sendCodec = sendCodec
976		return nil
977	}

979	// newFECSender builds a fresh FEC send plane (group encoder + optional adaptive controller)
980	// from the bind-wide fecCfg/adaptiveCfg, or returns (nil, nil) when FEC is disabled. Like
981	// newFECReceiver it reads only immutable post-construction config, so it is safe to call
982	// WITHOUT m.mu — which the lock-free re-instantiation on re-bind (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated)
983	// relies on to rebuild a torn-down concentrator peer's send plane symmetric to the receive
984	// plane. Open calls it too, so a lazily-rebuilt fecSend is byte-identical to an eagerly-opened
985	// one: a fresh encoder re-pinned to this bring-up, and in adaptive mode a fresh controller
986	// starting the control law from M=0 (no standing redundancy until loss is observed). A build
987	// error is a programmer error (the ratio/controller cfg were validated in NewMultipath).
988	func (m *Multipath) newFECSender() (*fecSender, error) {
989		if m.fecCfg == nil {
990			return nil, nil
991		}
992		enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(*m.fecCfg, fec.SystemClock{})
993		if err != nil {
994			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC encoder: %w", err)
995		}
996		fs := &fecSender{enc: enc}
997		if m.adaptiveCfg != nil {
998			ctrl, err := adaptivefec.NewController(*m.adaptiveCfg, adaptivefec.SystemClock{})
999			if err != nil {
1000				return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: build adaptive FEC controller: %w", err)
1001			}
1002			fs.ctrl = ctrl
1003			// Adopt the controller's starting parity so encoder and controller agree from t=0; the
1004			// tick loop sizes it to measured loss within the first control interval. Fixed mode
1005			// leaves the encoder at its cfg.ParityShards default instead.
1006			enc.SetParity(ctrl.Parity())
1007		}
1008		return fs, nil
1009	}
1010	
1011	// newFECReceiver builds a fresh FEC receive plane (decoder + residual-loss estimator) from
1012	// the bind-wide fecCfg, or returns (nil, nil) when FEC is disabled. It reads only immutable
1013	// post-construction config (m.fecCfg), so it is safe to call WITHOUT m.mu — which the
1014	// lock-free lazy receive-side instantiation on first authenticated binding
1015	// (ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated) relies on. The decoder tuning matches what Open pins for
1016	// the primary: a retain window, and a recovery deadline past which a doomed group is folded
1017	// into the unrecoverable counter (D24), so a lazily-instantiated concentrator peer recovers
1018	// identically to an eagerly-opened one.
1019	func (m *Multipath) newFECReceiver() (*fecReceiver, error) {
1020		if m.fecCfg == nil {
1021			return nil, nil
1022		}
1023		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(*m.fecCfg)
1024		if err != nil {
1025			return nil, fmt.Errorf("bind: build FEC decoder: %w", err)
1026		}
1027		dec.SetRetainWindow(fecRetainGroups)
1028		dec.SetClock(fec.SystemClock{})
1029		dec.SetRecoveryDeadline(m.fecCfg.Deadline + resequencerTimeout)
1030		return &fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)}, nil
1031	}
1032	
1033	// ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated lazily builds a peer's HEAVY receive-side datapath — the
1034	// ~2048-frame resequencer ring and (when FEC is configured) the decoder's per-group buffers —
1035	// on the FIRST authenticated source->peer binding, rather than eagerly at Open (Q26). A
1036	// configured concentrator peer that has never been reached therefore carries none of that
1037	// per-peer memory; it materialises only once an authenticated PROBE has bound a source to it,
1038	// and is reclaimed on teardown (teardownPeerLocked), re-materialising cleanly on the next
1039	// re-bind. It publishes through the same atomic.Pointer the receive fast path Loads and runs
1040	// on the Bind-owned readLoop goroutine, which must never take m.mu (Close waits on the readers
1041	// WHILE holding it) — so it takes ONLY the per-peer lifecycleMu, never m.mu. That lifecycleMu
1042	// makes the whole build-and-publish of the heavy trio (resequencer + FEC receive AND send
1043	// planes) mutually exclusive with teardownPeerLocked's clearing of the same trio: a teardown
1044	// can no longer interleave between the resequencer publish and the FEC publish and leave a
1045	// half-published plane, nor resurrect a plane on a torn-down peer. It is idempotent (the
1046	// lifecycleMu-guarded resequencer==nil double-check elects a single instantiator when two
1047	// sockets bind the same peer concurrently; the loser returns without building). It re-instates
1048	// the SEND plane symmetric to the receive plane, so a torn-down-then-rebound FEC peer sends
1049	// parity again rather than silently sending unprotected. A build error on either FEC plane is a
1050	// programmer error (the ratios were validated in NewMultipath) — the resequencer is still
1051	// installed so DATA flows, only FEC is absent, which is the safe degradation.
1052	func (m *Multipath) ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(ps *peerState) {
1053		if ps.resequencer.Load() != nil {
1054			return // fast path: already instantiated (the eager primary, or a prior binding)
1055		}
1056		ps.lifecycleMu.Lock()
1057		defer ps.lifecycleMu.Unlock()
1058		if ps.resequencer.Load() != nil {
1059			return // a concurrent bind instantiated it while we waited on lifecycleMu
1060		}
1061		// Build both FEC planes BEFORE publishing anything; on a build error degrade to no-FEC (the
1062		// resequencer alone still carries DATA). Store the resequencer LAST: it is the election
1063		// sentinel the fast-path check above and the receive fast path key on, so a concurrent
1064		// reader never observes a live resequencer whose FEC planes are not yet published.
1065		fr, ferr := m.newFECReceiver()
1066		if ferr != nil {
1067			fr = nil
1068		}
1069		fs, serr := m.newFECSender()
1070		if serr != nil {
1071			fs = nil
1072		}
1073		if fr != nil {
1074			ps.fecRecv.Store(fr)
1075		}
1076		if fs != nil {
1077			ps.fecSend.Store(fs)
1078		}
1079		ps.resequencer.Store(reseq.New(resequencerWindow, resequencerTimeout, reseq.SystemClock{}))
1080	}

1	package fec
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"sort"
6		"time"
7	
8		"github.com/klauspost/reedsolomon"
9	)
10	
11	// Decoder reassembles FEC groups from surviving shards and reconstructs data
12	// frames lost in transit. For any group that loses at most K of its M+K shards,
13	// the Decoder returns every data frame that was not directly received. It is
14	// content-agnostic and NOT safe for concurrent use.
15	//
16	// Recovery requires at least one surviving ParityShard, which conveys the group
17	// cardinality M and the uniform shard length. This is never a real limitation:
18	// whenever a DATA frame is actually lost, at least one of the K parity frames
19	// survives (a loss of D>=1 data frames within the <=K budget leaves K-(losses-D)
20	// >= 1 parity), so a group that needs recovery always has the metadata to do it.
21	// A group that loses only parity keeps all its data and needs no recovery.
22	type Decoder struct {
23		cfg    Config
24		codecs map[int]reedsolomon.Encoder
25		groups map[GroupID]*groupState
26	
27		// retainWindow bounds memory: groupState for a group more than this many
28		// GroupIDs behind the highest offered group is evicted (see tooOld). 0 disables
29		// the window entirely, leaving eviction to explicit Forget calls.
30		retainWindow  int
31		highWater     GroupID
32		haveHighWater bool
33	
34		// Discontinuity guard over the (UNAUTHENTICATED) wire GroupID, mirroring the
35		// resequencer's suspect/corroborate design (internal/reseq/reseq.go). DATA/PARITY
36		// frames are forgeable, so junk decodes as a valid kind ~1/256 carrying a
37		// uniformly-random GroupID; a single such frame must NOT be trusted to move the
38		// high-water arbitrarily (which would evict every live group and blackhole all
39		// subsequent real groups as tooOld). A GroupID implausibly far from the high-water
40		// is SUSPECT: it neither advances the high-water nor is buffered until
41		// groupResyncCorroborate DISTINCT suspect ids mutually within one retain window
42		// corroborate a genuine discontinuity (a peer encoder reopen resets nextGroup to 0,
43		// emitting a connected run of low ids that corroborates; independent junk ids, each
44		// uniform in 2^32, do not). resyncGroups holds the current run's distinct ids;
45		// [resyncLo,resyncHi] is its span.
46		resyncGroups []GroupID
47		resyncLo     GroupID
48		resyncHi     GroupID
49	
50		// recovered counts data shards reconstructed from parity (never directly
51		// received); unrecoverable counts data shards a group lost for good — its
52		// cardinality was known (a parity frame was seen) yet fewer than M shards
53		// survived, so the group is evicted from the retain window still incomplete.
54		// Both are cumulative over the decoder's life and read via Stats; the T24
55		// datapath surfaces them on /metrics.
56		recovered     uint64
57		unrecoverable uint64
58	
59		// clock and recoveryDeadline drive quiescence-accurate unrecoverable accounting
60		// (D24). The group-count-only eviction folds an incomplete group into
61		// unrecoverable ONLY when the retain window's high-water advances past it, and the
62		// high-water advances only when NEW groups are offered. At quiescence (traffic
63		// stops, a link stalls) the last retainWindow doomed groups are never evicted, so
64		// unrecoverable under-reports and /metrics OVERSTATES recovery exactly after an
65		// incident. To fix this WITHOUT perturbing the reconstruction buffer, Stats folds in
66		// every retained-incomplete group whose recovery deadline has DEFINITIVELY passed
67		// (clock.Now()-firstSeen >= recoveryDeadline: past the point any further parity for
68		// the group could still arrive). recoveryDeadline == 0 disables this snapshot-time
69		// accounting, leaving the pure group-window behaviour (the default; the T24 datapath
70		// sets a non-zero deadline via SetRecoveryDeadline). clock defaults to SystemClock.
71		clock            Clock
72		recoveryDeadline time.Duration
73	}
74	
75	// GroupID discontinuity-guard tuning, mirroring reseq's resyncFactor/resyncCorroborate.
76	const (
77		// groupResyncFactor bounds how far AHEAD of the high-water a single frame is
78		// trusted to advance it. The encoder emits group ids strictly sequentially, so a
79		// legitimate forward step is tiny (a handful, from fully-lost intervening groups);
80		// a jump of groupResyncFactor retain-windows or more is SUSPECT and a single such
81		// frame never advances the high-water. This is the group-space analogue of reseq's
82		// forward-jump bound.
83		groupResyncFactor = 4
84		// groupResyncCorroborate is how many DISTINCT suspect group ids mutually within one
85		// retain window must be observed before the high-water re-pins. A peer encoder
86		// reopen (ids restart at 0) emits a connected run that corroborates after losing at
87		// most groupResyncCorroborate-1 groups; uniformly-random junk ids never corroborate.
88		groupResyncCorroborate = 3
89	)
90	
91	// DecoderStats is a snapshot of the decoder's cumulative recovery counters.
92	type DecoderStats struct {
93		// Recovered is the number of data shards reconstructed from parity.
94		Recovered uint64
95		// Unrecoverable is the number of data shards lost beyond repair capacity — the
96		// per-group missing count of a group evicted from the retain window before it
97		// could be completed (its M was known from a parity frame but < M shards
98		// survived). A group whose parity was ALSO entirely lost (M never learned) is
99		// not counted here: with no surviving parity the decoder has no information
100		// about the group's cardinality, and the loss is accounted downstream by the
101		// resequencer's gap timeout instead.
102		Unrecoverable uint64
103	}
104	
105	// Stats returns a snapshot of the decoder's cumulative recovery counters. Before
106	// snapshotting it folds in every retained-incomplete group whose recovery deadline
107	// has definitively passed (accountExpired, D24), so at quiescence — when no new
108	// group is offered to advance the eviction high-water — a doomed tail group is
109	// counted unrecoverable promptly rather than only once ~retainWindow later groups
110	// arrive. Each group is counted exactly once (accountExpired marks it accounted, and
111	// the later window eviction skips an already-accounted group). This makes Stats a
112	// read-with-side-effects; the datapath serializes it with Offer under the receiver's
113	// mutex, matching the decoder's single-writer discipline.
114	func (d *Decoder) Stats() DecoderStats {
115		d.accountExpired()
116		return DecoderStats{Recovered: d.recovered, Unrecoverable: d.unrecoverable}
117	}
118	
119	// accountEviction folds a group being dropped from the retain window into the
120	// unrecoverable counter: a group whose cardinality M is known (a parity frame was

8	)
9	
10	// Encoder groups opaque DATA payloads into Reed-Solomon coding groups and emits
11	// parity. Two events close a group and produce its K parity shards:
12	//
13	//   - Admit fills the group to N data shards, or
14	//   - Tick observes that the grouping Deadline has elapsed since the group opened
15	//     (a partially-filled group is coded over its current M<N data shards).
16	//
17	// The Encoder is content-agnostic: it treats each payload as opaque bytes and
18	// never inspects the wrapped datagram. It is NOT safe for concurrent use; the
19	// datapath drives one Encoder from a single goroutine.
20	type Encoder struct {
21		cfg   Config
22		clock Clock
23	
24		// codecs caches one Reed-Solomon codec per (data-shard-count, parity-count)
25		// pair. The data count varies with the group fill (full N, or a deadline-flushed
26		// partial M in [1,N]); the parity count is cfg.ParityShards in fixed mode but
27		// varies per group in [0,cfg.ParityShards] when the adaptive controller drives it
28		// via SetParity (T29), so the key spans both dimensions.
29		codecs map[codecKey]reedsolomon.Encoder
30	
31		// targetParity is the parity count the NEXT opened group will use. It is
32		// cfg.ParityShards at construction (fixed-ratio behaviour) and is retargeted by
33		// SetParity as the adaptive controller resizes redundancy (T29). It is read once
34		// per group at openGroup and clamped to [0,cfg.ParityShards] there.
